profileNextAction.complianceAttorney: Your status history can affect your options — a qualified immigration attorney can advise on next steps.
profileNextAction.protection: Protection cases are time-sensitive — consider speaking with an immigration attorney or an accredited representative.
profileNextAction.naturalizationEligible: You may be eligible to apply for U.S. citizenship — review the N-400 requirements to confirm.
profileNextAction.naturalizationSoon: You're approaching citizenship eligibility — a good time to start gathering your N-400 documents.
profileNextAction.gcMaintain: Keep your green card valid and track your time toward citizenship eligibility.
profileNextAction.gcPending: Your green card application is in progress — keep your case tracked for updates.
profileNextAction.expirySoon: Plan your renewal or next step before your status expires.
profileNextAction.consularNext: With your petition moving forward, prepare for your consular interview and the documents it requires.
profileNextAction.expiryPlanning: Nothing urgent right now — keep an eye on your status expiry and plan ahead.

templates.received: USCIS has received this case and it is now in their system. A final decision does not appear to have been made yet. There is usually nothing to do at this stage — watch for official notices and keep your mailing address with USCIS current.
templates.under_review: USCIS appears to be actively reviewing this case. This usually means it is still pending and no final decision has been made yet. Processing times vary, so a case can stay in this stage for a while.
templates.biometrics: This status usually relates to a biometrics (fingerprint) appointment. If one has been scheduled, the official notice will list the date, time, and location. Read that notice carefully and note the details.
templates.interview: This status usually relates to an interview. If one has been scheduled, the official notice will explain when and where to appear and what to bring. Read that notice carefully and note the date.
templates.action_required: USCIS may need more information or a response before it can continue with this case. Read the official notice carefully — it explains what is being requested and the deadline to respond. Pay close attention to any dates, as missing a deadline can affect a case.
templates.transferred: This case appears to have been transferred to another USCIS office, often to help with processing. A transfer by itself does not usually indicate a problem. Watch for further notices, which may now come from the new office.
templates.approved: USCIS appears to have approved this case. Depending on the type of case there may be further steps — such as a document being produced or mailed, or a stage handled outside USCIS. Read the official notice for what comes next.
templates.document_production: A card or document related to this case appears to be in production or on its way. Watch for delivery updates and keep your mailing address with USCIS current so it reaches you.
templates.denied: USCIS appears to have denied this case. The official notice explains the reason and describes any options that may be available, such as an appeal or motion, along with any deadline that applies. Read it carefully and pay attention to any dates.
templates.rejected: This filing appears to have been rejected, which usually means USCIS did not accept it for processing (for example, an issue with the fee, signature, or form edition). The official notice explains why. In some situations a filing can be corrected and resubmitted — read the notice carefully to understand what it says and any timeframe.
templates.closed: This case appears to be closed and is no longer active. This can happen for several reasons, including a request to withdraw it. The official notice or record explains the details.
templates.unknown: OnePath found this case status, but it does not match a common guidance category yet. Read the official USCIS status shown above carefully and watch for any official notices. The status shown is always the authoritative source.

blend.label: For your situation
blend.approved_i140_backlog: Your employment green-card petition (I-140) appears approved — this secures your place in line, but it is not the green card itself. Because your country has a visa backlog, there can still be a wait before you can file the green-card application. Check the Visa Bulletin against your priority date.
blend.approved_i140: Your employment green-card petition (I-140) appears approved. This secures your place in line — it is not the green card itself. The next step is usually the green-card application (I-485) or consular processing, depending on your priority date.
blend.approved_i130: Your family petition (I-130) appears approved. This is a step toward the green card, not the green card itself — what comes next depends on your category and priority date.
blend.approved_adjustment: If this is your adjustment-of-status case (I-485), an approval can mean lawful permanent residence — a green card. The official notice, and the card that follows, confirm it.
blend.approved_naturalization: Your naturalization case (N-400) appears approved. The usual final step is the oath ceremony — watch for a notice with the date, time, and location.
blend.approved_remove_conditions: Your petition to remove conditions on residence (I-751) appears approved — this typically leads to a 10-year green card. Watch for the new card in the mail.
blend.approved_ead: Your work-permit application (I-765) appears approved. Check the official notice or the EAD card for the exact validity dates before relying on it.
blend.approved_advance_parole: Your travel-document application (Advance Parole, I-131) appears approved. Confirm its validity dates before any international travel.
blend.approved_employer_petition: Your employer's petition (I-129) appears approved. If you are outside the US you may still need a visa before traveling; if you are in the US it may extend or change your status. The official notice explains which applies.
blend.approved_extend_change: Your request to extend or change status (I-539) appears approved. Check the official notice for your new status and its dates.
blend.docprod_greencard: Your green card appears to be in production. Keep your mailing address with USCIS current so it reaches you.
blend.docprod_ead: Your work-permit (EAD) card appears to be in production. Keep your address with USCIS current, and check the card's validity dates when it arrives.
blend.denied_adjustment: A denial of your adjustment case (I-485) can also affect a work permit or travel document tied to it. Read the official notice's reason, options, and deadlines carefully.
blend.denied_ead: A denial of your work-permit application (I-765) can affect your ability to work. Read the official notice for the reason and any options or deadlines.
blend.denied_employer_petition: This employer petition (I-129) appears denied, which can affect status that depends on it. The official notice explains any options and deadlines.
blend.denied_naturalization: A naturalization denial does not remove your green card. The official notice explains the reason, whether you may reapply or appeal, and any deadline.

summary.opt_india: You're on OPT — time to find an H-1B sponsor. The FY2027 lottery registration ran March 4–19, 2026. Note: USCIS now uses a wage-weighted selection process that favors higher-paid positions. India has significant EB-2/EB-3 backlogs (12+ years).

summary.insideUs: Asylum must be filed within 1 year of arrival. You can apply for work authorization 150 days after filing. Note: EAD validity for asylum applicants is now 18 months max, and auto-extensions have been eliminated. The process is complex — legal representation strongly recommended.

summary.over5: You meet the 5-year continuous residence requirement and can file Form N-400 now. You'll need to pass an English language test and a civics exam (100 questions). Processing typically takes 8–14 months from filing to oath ceremony.
summary.3to5: If you are married to and living with a U.S. citizen, you may qualify under the 3-year rule. This requires 18 months of physical presence and continuous residence during those 3 years. Otherwise, you'll need to wait until the 5-year mark.
summary.2to3: You're getting close to eligibility. The standard route requires 5 years as a green card holder, but if you're married to a U.S. citizen, you may qualify after just 3 years. Use this time to prepare your documents and study for the civics test.
summary.under2: You're not yet eligible to naturalize, but now is a great time to prepare. Track your travel history, maintain your green card in good standing, and start studying for the 100-question civics exam.
summary.default: Naturalization is the process of becoming a U.S. citizen. Most green card holders are eligible after 5 years of continuous residence. If you're married to a U.S. citizen, you may qualify after just 3 years.

default.title: Your Immigration Journey
default.summary: Let's explore your options based on your current situation.

H1B.deadlineUrgent: File TODAY — employer must act immediately
H1B.deadlineSoon: File at least 6 months before expiry
H1B.tip: Premium processing gets 15-day decision. Your employer files, not you.

F1.deadlineUrgent: Contact DSO today — grace period is only 60 days after program end
F1.tip: Your Designated School Official (DSO) must extend your I-20 before you can file anything.

OPT.deadlineUrgent: File immediately — apply up to 90 days before EAD expires
OPT.deadlineSoon: Apply now — USCIS processing takes 3-5 months
OPT.tip: Must have STEM degree and E-Verify employer. File before current EAD expires or you lose work auth.

L1.tip: L-1A max 7 years total, L-1B max 5 years. Premium gets 15-day decision.

J1.purpose: Extend your J-1 exchange visitor program
J1.deadlineSoon: Contact sponsor 3 months before end date
J1.tip: Only your program sponsor can extend the DS-2019. You cannot file this yourself.

B1B2.deadlineUrgent: File immediately — overstay has serious consequences
B1B2.deadlineSoon: File at least 45 days before I-94 expires
B1B2.tip: Must file BEFORE your I-94 expires. Overstaying even one day can affect future visa applications.

EAD.deadlineUrgent: File immediately — EAD validity now 18 months max
EAD.deadlineSoon: File 6 months before expiry — processing takes 3-5 months
EAD.tip: Auto-extensions eliminated as of Oct 2025. File early — a gap in EAD means you cannot work legally.

GC_pending.purpose: Renew your EAD/Advance Parole combo card
GC_pending.deadlineUrgent: File immediately — do not let combo card expire
GC_pending.tip: Traveling without valid Advance Parole while I-485 is pending can abandon your green card application.

findReceipt.title: Find your receipt number first
findReceipt.summary: USCIS issues a receipt number when they accept your petition. You'll need it to track the case in this app and on the USCIS website. Most people can find it within 30 days of filing.
findReceipt.primaryAction: How to find your receipt number
findReceipt.tipI797: 💡 Your receipt number is on the Form I-797 Notice of Action that USCIS mails after accepting your petition. It looks like 3 letters + 10 digits (example: IOE0123456789).
findReceipt.recCheckEmail: 📧 If you used USCIS online filing, check your USCIS account or the confirmation email
findReceipt.recCheckSpouse: 👥 If a relative or attorney filed on your behalf, ask them for the receipt number
findReceipt.recReturnLater: ⏳ Petitions usually generate a receipt within 2–4 weeks of filing — come back when you have it

warnings.h1bCap: The FY2027 H-1B registration period closes March 19, 2026. A new wage-weighted lottery favors higher-paid positions.
warnings.h1bWeightedLottery: New for FY2027: USCIS uses a weighted selection process. Higher wage levels have significantly better odds of selection. Entry-level positions face reduced chances.
warnings.h1bFeeAlert: Presidential Proclamation 10973: A $100,000 additional fee applies to certain H-1B petitions requiring consular processing (beneficiary outside the U.S.). This is in addition to all regular filing fees.
warnings.h1bPremiumIncrease: Premium processing fees increased effective March 1, 2026. I-129/I-140: now $2,965. I-765: now $1,780. I-539: now $2,075.
warnings.familyLongWait: Some family preference categories have multi-year waiting periods due to annual visa limits. F4 siblings can wait 15+ years for certain countries.
warnings.familyPolicyShifts: Presidential Proclamations have reduced visa issuance rates for certain nationalities. Dates have been advanced for other countries but retrogression is possible later in FY2026.
warnings.indiaEbBacklog: India EB-2 final action date: September 15, 2013. EB-3: November 15, 2013. The backlog remains 12+ years. Some forward movement occurred in early 2026.
warnings.chinaEbBacklog: China EB-2 final action date: September 1, 2021. EB-3: May 1, 2021. Multi-year backlog persists for mainland-born applicants.
warnings.eadValidityReduced: Effective December 5, 2025: EAD maximum validity reduced from 5 years to 18 months for adjustment of status applicants, refugees, asylees, and related categories. File renewals early — gaps in work authorization are now more likely.
warnings.eadAutoExtensionEnded: Effective October 30, 2025: Automatic 540-day EAD extensions have been eliminated for most categories. EADs now expire on their printed date. Plan renewals at least 6 months ahead.
warnings.eadTpsParoleOneYear: Per H.R. 1 (One Big Beautiful Bill Act): TPS and parole EADs are now valid for a maximum of 1 year or the end of the authorized period, whichever is shorter.
warnings.asylumVolatility: Asylum policy is subject to active changes including enhanced screening rules and proposed regulations. Treat this as a high-volatility pathway and consult an attorney for current guidance.
warnings.studentOptPlanning: Apply for OPT up to 90 days before graduation. STEM OPT requires E-Verify employer. Processing currently takes ~4.5 months — plan accordingly.
warnings.processingTimesDisclaimer: USCIS processing times have slowed significantly in recent months. Actual times may exceed posted estimates. Consider premium processing where available.
warnings.dvScam: DV Lottery scam alert: the only official entry costs $1 at the government site. Anyone charging more, promising to boost your odds, or emailing to 'confirm you won' is running a scam.
warnings.dvPaused: DV visa issuance is currently paused pending a federal review. Entry may still open, but issuance timing is uncertain — verify at travel.state.gov before relying on it.

pathwayViability.disclaimer: Viability assessments are general guidance based on current policy and processing conditions. Individual circumstances vary. Consult an immigration attorney for personalized advice.

HIGH.label: High Viability
HIGH.description: Structurally stable path with predictable process

CONDITIONAL.label: Conditional
CONDITIONAL.description: Viable for the right candidate, but narrower or competitive

LOWER.label: Lower / Volatile
LOWER.description: Significant barriers, long waits, or policy uncertainty

H1B.shortReason: Cap competition is high. New weighted lottery favors higher wages.

details.d1: FY2027 introduces wage-weighted selection — entry-level positions face reduced odds.
details.d2: $100,000 additional fee for certain consular-processing petitions.
details.d3: Registration fee increased to $215. Premium processing now $2,965.
details.d4: Demand consistently exceeds the 85,000 annual cap by 4–8x.

H1B.recommendation: Best for candidates offered wages at OEWS Level III or IV. Consider O-1 or L-1 alternatives if eligible.

L1.shortReason: Not cap-subject. Good for qualifying multinational employees.

details.d1: Not subject to the H-1B annual cap or lottery.
details.d2: Requires 1 year of employment with qualifying multinational company.
details.d3: Narrower eligibility, but avoids the cap-season bottleneck.

L1.recommendation: High viability for the right candidate. If you've worked for a multinational for 1+ year, this may be stronger than H-1B.

O1.shortReason: No cap, but requires extraordinary ability evidence.

details.d1: Not subject to annual cap — can file any time.
details.d2: Requires documented extraordinary ability or achievement.
details.d3: Strategically attractive for founders, researchers, creatives, athletes.
details.d4: Harder to qualify for, but more predictable than H-1B if eligible.

O1.recommendation: If you have awards, publications, media coverage, or high salary evidence, explore this before H-1B.

IMMEDIATE_RELATIVE.shortReason: Strongest family path. Not subject to preference backlog system.

details.d1: Immediate relatives of U.S. citizens are not constrained by per-country limits.
details.d2: Includes spouses, unmarried children under 21, and parents of adult citizens.
details.d3: Processing times are measured in months, not years (unlike preference categories).

IMMEDIATE_RELATIVE.recommendation: If you qualify as an immediate relative, this is one of the most reliable paths available.

FAMILY_PREFERENCE.shortReason: Backlogs of 5–20+ years depending on category and country.

details.d1: F1, F2B, F3, F4 categories all face multi-year waits.
details.d2: Mexico and Philippines face the longest backlogs (some 20+ years).
details.d3: Presidential Proclamations have further impacted issuance rates for certain nationalities.
details.d4: Retrogression is possible later in FY2026.

FAMILY_PREFERENCE.recommendation: File early to establish a priority date, but plan for a long wait. Consider whether other pathways are available in parallel.

F1_STUDENT.shortReason: Stable entry path. OPT/STEM OPT provide a work bridge.

details.d1: F-1 status, OPT, and 24-month STEM OPT extension remain active and structurally intact.
details.d2: Not a direct immigrant path, but a realistic 'enter, study, work, then transition' route.
details.d3: Embassy wait times for F-1 are generally shorter than other visa types.

F1_STUDENT.recommendation: One of the most practical entry paths for younger applicants. Plan your STEM major carefully for the 36-month OPT window.

EB_GENERAL.shortReason: Viable for most countries, but India/China face severe backlogs.

details.d1: Rest-of-world EB-2 and EB-3 are relatively current or have short waits.
details.d2: India EB-2: final action date Sep 15, 2013 (12+ year backlog).
details.d3: China EB-2: final action date Sep 1, 2021 (4+ year backlog).
details.d4: Per-country limits create massive disparities in wait times.

EB_GENERAL.recommendation: For non-backlogged countries, this is a strong path. For India/China, plan for a very long wait and explore alternatives like EB-1 or NIW.

ASYLUM.shortReason: Policy environment is volatile. Enhanced screening in effect.

details.d1: Active policy changes including proposed rules for stronger screening.
details.d2: EAD validity for asylum applicants reduced to 18 months.
details.d4: Should not be treated as a stable 'strategy' — it's a protection pathway for those in genuine need.

ASYLUM.recommendation: If you have a legitimate fear of persecution, seek legal counsel immediately. Do not rely on asylum as a planned immigration strategy.

CONSULAR_PROCESSING.shortReason: Trackable process through NVC for qualifying family relationships.

details.d1: State Department's NVC and IV scheduling tools make this path more trackable.
details.d2: Structurally stable for immediate relatives and qualifying family/employment cases.
details.d3: Can be slow, but the pipeline is visible through official tools.
details.d4: Presidential Proclamations have impacted processing for certain nationalities.

CONSULAR_PROCESSING.recommendation: If abroad with a qualifying petition approved, this is a well-documented and trackable path.

DV.shortReason: Costs $1 to enter, but odds are very low and issuance is currently paused.

details.d1: The official registration fee is $1. Anyone charging more is a scam — there are no paid shortcuts or ways to improve your odds.
details.d2: Visa issuance is currently paused pending a federal review of screening procedures. Verify current status at travel.state.gov.
details.d3: High-immigration countries are excluded each year — including Jamaica, Haiti, Cuba, and the Dominican Republic. The list changes annually.
details.d4: Selection does not guarantee a green card — selectees still compete for a limited number of visas before the cycle closes.
details.d5: Eligibility is by country of birth, not citizenship; a spouse's birth country may also qualify you.

DV.recommendation: Worth entering if you're from an eligible country — it's $1 — but treat it as a long shot, not a primary plan. Never pay a third party to enter for you.

E2.shortReason: Stable and renewable, but requires treaty nationality and a substantial investment.

details.d1: Only nationals of E-2 treaty countries qualify. In the Caribbean: Grenada, Jamaica, Trinidad & Tobago, and Suriname.
details.d2: You must invest a substantial amount of your own capital in a real, operating U.S. business and be able to develop and direct it.
details.d3: Renewable in 2-year increments with no limit — but it never leads directly to a green card.
details.d4: Your spouse can apply for work authorization; children under 21 can study.
details.d5: Citizenship obtained through an investment (CBI) program requires 3 years of residence there first (AMIGOS Act, 2022), and CBI programs face increased U.S. scrutiny.

E2.recommendation: Worth exploring if you're a treaty-country national with capital to invest in a U.S. business. Use an immigration attorney to structure the investment correctly.

H2B.shortReason: Real seasonal route, but employer-driven and heavily oversubscribed.

details.d1: You cannot self-petition — a U.S. employer must sponsor you for a specific seasonal or temporary job.
details.d2: The statutory cap is 66,000 per year, split between the two halves, and demand routinely exceeds it within weeks.
details.d3: For FY2026, up to 64,716 supplemental visas were added on top of the cap — but this extra authority is approved year-by-year and isn't guaranteed to continue.
details.d4: Returning workers (those who held H-2B status in recent years) get priority in most supplemental allocations.
details.d5: There is no direct path to a green card, and family on H-4 cannot work.

H2B.recommendation: Strong option if you can line up a seasonal U.S. employer willing to sponsor — especially as a returning worker. Apply early; allocations fill fast.

durationOfStatusPending.title: Coming: a fixed stay period for F-1 and J-1 (not in effect yet)
durationOfStatusPending.summary: A new rule would replace 'duration of status' with a fixed end date on your I-94. It has cleared review but is not yet law — nothing has changed for you today.
durationOfStatusPending.details: For decades, F-1 and J-1 visa holders have been admitted for 'duration of status' — you may stay as long as you follow the terms of your status. DHS has finalized a rule that would replace this with a fixed end date and require a formal extension application to stay longer. The rule cleared White House review on June 17, 2026, but it has not been published in the Federal Register, and it would take effect 60 days after it is. Until then, the current rules — including your current grace period — still apply. Some news reports describe the proposed terms as though they were already law. They are not, and the final terms may still change.

actions.a1: Nothing has changed today. Your current status and grace period still apply.
actions.a2: Talk to your DSO or program sponsor before making plans around your program end date.
actions.a3: Watch official ICE and SEVP announcements for the final rule — do not rely on news summaries.

impactByStatus.F1: This would affect you. Nothing has changed yet — check with your DSO before acting on anything you read about it.
impactByStatus.OPT: This would affect you, and may change how you extend your stay during OPT. Nothing has changed yet.
impactByStatus.J1: This would affect you. Nothing has changed yet — check with your program sponsor.

tpsTerminationsScotus.title: Supreme Court: TPS Terminations Cannot Be Challenged in Court
tpsTerminationsScotus.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that federal courts cannot review DHS decisions to end TPS. Terminations for Haiti and Syria are moving forward, and other countries are affected.
tpsTerminationsScotus.details: On June 25, 2026, in Mullin v. Doe, the Supreme Court held that federal courts cannot review the Secretary of Homeland Security's decision to end a country's TPS designation. The terminations for Haiti and Syria move forward. USCIS is extending TPS work permits for affected countries only in short increments, so expiration dates are changing week to week. Designations that remain active are also more vulnerable, because the legal route to challenge a termination is now largely closed.

actions.a1: Check the official USCIS TPS page for your country for the current work-permit expiration date — it may have changed since you last looked.
actions.a2: Speak with an immigration attorney or accredited representative now about other status options.
actions.a3: Do not rely on an expiration date you saw previously, or on the date printed on your card.

impactByStatus.EAD: If your work permit is based on TPS, it may end sooner than the date printed on it. Verify the current date with USCIS and get legal advice right away.

h1b100kFee.title: $100,000 H-1B Fee — Consular Processing
h1b100kFee.summary: Additional $100,000 payment required for certain H-1B petitions approved for consular processing.
h1b100kFee.details: Applies when the beneficiary is outside the U.S. or when the petition is approved for consular processing. Generally does NOT apply to F-1→H-1B change of status within the U.S. Total employer cost can exceed $106,000.

actions.a1: Determine if beneficiary qualifies for change of status (avoids fee)
actions.a2: Consider L-1 or O-1 alternatives if eligible
actions.a3: Budget accordingly if consular processing is required

impactByStatus.F1: MODERATE — F-1→H-1B change of status generally avoids this fee
impactByStatus.OPT: MODERATE — Cap-gap eligible OPT holders can file change of status

eadValidity18Months.title: EAD Validity Reduced to 18 Months
eadValidity18Months.summary: Maximum EAD validity cut from 5 years to 18 months for adjustment-of-status applicants, refugees, asylees, and related categories.
eadValidity18Months.details: Affects pending and new I-765 applications filed on or after Dec 5, 2025. Does NOT affect OPT, STEM OPT, H-4, J-2, or L-2 EADs. Existing EADs retain their printed expiration date.

actions.a1: File EAD renewals at least 6 months before expiration
actions.a2: No automatic 540-day extensions — plan for potential work gaps

impactByStatus.GC_pending: CRITICAL — Must renew EAD every 18 months
impactByStatus.EAD: HIGH — Check which category your EAD falls under

eadAutoExtensionEnded.title: 540-Day EAD Auto-Extensions Eliminated
eadAutoExtensionEnded.summary: Automatic 540-day EAD extensions ended for most categories. EADs now expire on the printed date.
eadAutoExtensionEnded.details: Previously, timely-filed EAD renewals automatically extended the old EAD for 540 days. This is no longer the case. If USCIS hasn't adjudicated your renewal by your EAD's expiration date, you must stop working.

actions.a1: File renewals as early as possible (6 months before expiration)
actions.a2: Consider premium processing for I-765 if available ($1,780)

h1bWeightedLottery.title: H-1B Wage-Weighted Lottery (FY2027+)
h1bWeightedLottery.summary: USCIS replaced the random H-1B lottery with a wage-weighted selection system. Higher wages = better odds.
h1bWeightedLottery.details: Level I (entry) wages get 1x odds. Level IV (expert) wages get up to 4x odds. Registration must include SOC code and OEWS wage level. First applied to FY2027 registration (March 4-19, 2026).

actions.a1: Offer competitive wages to maximize selection odds
actions.a2: Ensure correct SOC code and wage level on registration
actions.a3: Consider O-1 or L-1 if H-1B odds are unfavorable at your wage level

impactByStatus.OPT: HIGH — Entry-level offers face significantly reduced odds
impactByStatus.H1B: MODERATE — Affects transfers if new registration needed

actions.a1: Budget for higher premium processing costs

tpsParoleEadOneYear.details: Initial and renewal EADs for TPS beneficiaries and parolees are now valid for the shorter of 1 year or the end date of the authorized period. Implemented via Federal Register notice July 22, 2025.

actions.a2: Budget for more frequent filing fees

indiaEbBacklogUpdate.details: EB-2 India advanced from May 15, 2013 (Jan 2025) to Sep 15, 2013 (Mar 2026). EB-3 India advanced to Nov 15, 2013. Dates for Filing show further movement. April 2026 bulletin shows additional advances.

actions.a1: Maintain H-1B status — do not let it expire
actions.a2: File H-1B extensions based on approved I-140
actions.a4: Explore EB-5 as alternative if financially feasible

contingencyActions.a1: Budget for the fee. It is currently being collected for petitions that require consular processing.

contingencyActions.a3: Do not make plans that depend on the fee disappearing. Watch for updates before you decide.

contingencyActions.a1: Prepare for both random and weighted lottery outcomes
contingencyActions.a2: File at appropriate wage level regardless

pros.p2: Returning workers get priority access to most supplemental visas.

cons.c1: You cannot self-petition — you need an employer to sponsor you.
cons.c2: Heavily oversubscribed; allocations often fill within weeks.
cons.c3: No direct path to a green card.

f1.name: Form I-129 (Petition for a Nonimmigrant Worker)
f1.purpose: Filed by the U.S. employer to request H-2B classification for you.

f2.name: Form ETA-9142B (Temporary Labor Certification)
f2.purpose: The employer's DOL labor certification, required before the I-129.
f2.who: The sponsoring U.S. employer, filed with the Department of Labor.

f3.name: Form DS-160 (Nonimmigrant Visa Application)
f3.purpose: Your visa application at a U.S. consulate after the petition is approved.
f3.who: The worker, if applying from abroad.

E2.purpose: Lets nationals of treaty countries live in the U.S. to develop and direct a business in which they have invested a substantial amount of capital.
E2.note: Requires treaty nationality. A new $250 visa integrity fee is being phased in on top of the existing visa fee.
E2.duration: Up to 2 years per period, renewable indefinitely while the business operates.
E2.pathToGreenCard: No direct path — E-2 is a nonimmigrant status, even though it can be renewed indefinitely.
E2.spouseWork: Spouse can apply for work authorization; children under 21 can attend school but cannot work.

eligibility.e1: You are a national of a country that maintains an E-2 treaty with the U.S. (in the Caribbean: Grenada, Jamaica, Trinidad & Tobago, Suriname).
eligibility.e2: You have invested, or are actively investing, a substantial amount of your own capital in a bona fide U.S. enterprise.
eligibility.e3: The business is real and operating — not passive or speculative — and you will develop and direct it.
eligibility.e4: Citizenship acquired through an investment (CBI) program requires 3 years of residence in that country first (AMIGOS Act, 2022).

pros.p1: Renewable indefinitely as long as the business operates.
pros.p3: No fixed minimum investment, and no annual cap or lottery.

cons.c1: Limited to treaty-country nationals.
cons.c2: Requires a substantial, at-risk investment of your own funds.
cons.c3: No direct path to a green card.
cons.c4: CBI-acquired citizenship requires 3 years' residence first, and CBI programs face rising U.S. scrutiny.

f1.name: Form DS-160 (Nonimmigrant Visa Application)
f1.purpose: The visa application filed at a U.S. consulate (applicants abroad).

f2.name: Form DS-156E (Treaty Trader/Investor Application)
f2.purpose: Supplemental form documenting the treaty business and investment.

f3.name: Form I-129 (Petition for a Nonimmigrant Worker)
f3.purpose: Used to change to or extend E-2 status from within the U.S.

L1.purpose: For employees transferring from foreign branch to U.S. office of same company

eligibility.e1: Worked for company abroad for 1+ year in past 3 years
eligibility.e2: Manager/Executive (L-1A) or Specialized Knowledge (L-1B)
eligibility.e3: Company must have qualifying relationship

L1.duration: L-1A: 3 years initially (7 total), L-1B: 3 years (5 total)
L1.pathToGreenCard: Yes — L-1A can lead to EB-1C green card (faster than H-1B route)
L1.spouseWork: L-2 spouse can work with EAD immediately

pros.p1: No lottery or cap
pros.p4: L-1A to EB-1C is faster green card route

cons.c1: Must work for same employer
cons.c3: Requires prior employment abroad

O1.purpose: For individuals with extraordinary ability in sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics

eligibility.e1: Demonstrated extraordinary ability
eligibility.e2: National/international recognition
eligibility.e3: Substantial evidence of achievements
eligibility.e4: Job offer in field of expertise

O1.duration: Up to 3 years initially, unlimited 1-year extensions
O1.pathToGreenCard: Yes — can self-petition for EB-1A or employer can sponsor

pros.p1: No lottery or cap

cons.c1: High evidence threshold
cons.c3: Extensive documentation needed

EB.fullName: Employment-Based Green Card (EB-1, EB-2, EB-3, EB-4, EB-5)
EB.purpose: Direct path to permanent residency through employment, with five preference categories ranked by skill, education, or investment level

eligibility.e1: EB-1: Extraordinary ability, outstanding professors/researchers, or multinational executives (no PERM required)
eligibility.e2: EB-2: Advanced degree (master's+) or exceptional ability — National Interest Waiver allows self-petition
eligibility.e3: EB-3: Skilled workers (2+ years experience), professionals (bachelor's degree), or other workers
eligibility.e4: EB-4: Special immigrants (religious workers, special immigrant juveniles, certain government workers)
eligibility.e5: EB-5: Investor — minimum $800K in targeted employment area or $1.05M elsewhere, must create 10 jobs
eligibility.e6: Most categories require employer sponsorship and labor certification (PERM)

EB.currentWait: EB-1/EB-2/EB-3 ROW: 1-3 years; India/China EB-2/EB-3: 5-15+ years for priority date
EB.pathToGreenCard: This IS the green card — direct path to permanent residence
EB.spouseWork: Spouse and unmarried children under 21 receive green cards as derivatives

pros.p1: Direct path to permanent residence
pros.p2: EB-1 and EB-2 NIW allow self-petition (no employer needed)
pros.p5: EB-1A and NIW skip the lengthy PERM process

cons.c1: India and China face severe backlogs in EB-2/EB-3 (10+ years)
cons.c2: PERM labor certification adds 12-17 months for EB-2/EB-3 standard
cons.c3: Total filing fees: ~$3,000-$5,000+ per applicant
cons.c4: Most categories tie you to sponsoring employer until I-485 has been pending 180+ days
cons.c5: Visa bulletin retrogression can extend wait times unpredictably

EB.note: EB-1A (extraordinary ability) and EB-2 NIW are self-petition categories — you can file without an employer if you meet the high evidentiary bar.

f1.name: ETA-9089 (PERM)
f1.purpose: Labor certification (required for EB-2 and EB-3 standard, not NIW)

f2.name: Form I-140
f2.purpose: Immigrant Petition for Alien Worker — establishes EB classification

f3.name: Form I-485
f3.purpose: Adjustment of Status to permanent resident (if in U.S.)
f3.who: Beneficiary files when priority date is current

f4.name: Form DS-260
f4.purpose: Immigrant visa application (if outside U.S. — consular processing)

IR.purpose: For spouses, unmarried children under 21, and parents of U.S. citizens

eligibility.e1: Spouse of U.S. citizen (IR-1/CR-1)
eligibility.e2: Unmarried child under 21 of U.S. citizen (IR-2/CR-2)
eligibility.e3: Parent of U.S. citizen who is 21+ (IR-5)

IR.pathToGreenCard: This IS the green card — immediate permanent residence
IR.spouseWork: Yes — green card allows unrestricted work

pros.p1: No annual limits or waiting for priority date

cons.c1: Still takes 12-24 months processing
cons.c3: CR-1 conditional if married <2 years

IR.note: IMMEDIATE RELATIVE means no visa number wait — you skip the line! Other family categories wait years for visa numbers.

K1.purpose: Allows a foreign fiancé(e) of a U.S. citizen to enter the U.S. to marry within 90 days, then adjust to green card

eligibility.e1: Petitioner must be a U.S. citizen (not a green card holder)
eligibility.e2: Both parties must be legally free to marry
eligibility.e3: Must have met in person at least once within the 2 years before filing (waivers exist for cultural or hardship reasons)
eligibility.e4: U.S. citizen must meet income requirement (100% of Federal Poverty Guidelines)
eligibility.e5: Must intend to marry within 90 days of fiancé(e)'s arrival

K1.duration: Single entry, valid 6 months — must marry within 90 days of arrival
K1.currentWait: 10-12 months I-129F processing; 18-26 months total filing to green card
K1.pathToGreenCard: After marriage, file I-485 for adjustment of status — leads to 2-year conditional green card
K1.spouseWork: Can apply for EAD after arrival and again after I-485 filing

pros.p1: Allows fiancé(e) to enter U.S. before marriage
pros.p2: Children under 21 can come on K-2 derivative visas
pros.p3: Faster than waiting abroad for an IR-1/CR-1 spouse visa in some cases

cons.c1: Must marry within 90 days or fiancé(e) must leave
cons.c2: $675 I-129F fee (or $625 online) plus separate visa, medical, and adjustment fees (~$2,400+ total)
cons.c3: Initial green card is conditional — must file I-751 jointly within 90 days before 2-year anniversary
cons.c4: Cannot adjust through any other means if marriage to petitioner doesn't occur
cons.c5: If filing fails or relationship ends, fiancé(e) generally has no path to remain in U.S.

K1.note: The 90-day marriage deadline is strict — extensions are not available. Plan the wedding date before fiancé(e) travels.

f1.name: Form I-129F
f1.purpose: Petition for Alien Fiancé(e) — establishes the relationship
f1.who: U.S. citizen petitioner files with USCIS

f2.name: Form DS-160
f2.purpose: Online nonimmigrant visa application for the consular interview

f3.name: Form I-485
f3.purpose: Adjustment of Status after marriage to obtain green card

f4.name: Form I-134
f4.purpose: Affidavit of Support — proof petitioner can financially sponsor

F1.fullName: F-1: Unmarried Sons/Daughters of U.S. Citizens
F1.purpose: For unmarried children 21+ years old of U.S. citizens

eligibility.e1: Must be unmarried
eligibility.e2: 21 years or older
eligibility.e3: Child of U.S. citizen

F1.currentWait: 7-8 years from petition to visa availability
F1.pathToGreenCard: This leads directly to green card once visa number available

pros.p1: Leads to permanent residence

cons.c1: 7-8 year wait for visa number
cons.c3: Marriage terminates eligibility

F2A.fullName: F-2A: Spouses and Minor Children of Green Card Holders
F2A.purpose: For spouses and unmarried children under 21 of lawful permanent residents

eligibility.e1: Spouse of green card holder
eligibility.e2: Unmarried child under 21 of LPR

F2A.currentWait: 2-3 years from petition to visa availability
F2A.pathToGreenCard: This leads directly to green card

pros.p1: Shorter wait than other family preferences

cons.c1: 2-3 year wait for visa number

F2B.fullName: F-2B: Unmarried Adult Children of Green Card Holders
F2B.purpose: For unmarried children 21+ of lawful permanent residents

eligibility.e1: Unmarried
eligibility.e2: 21 years or older
eligibility.e3: Child of green card holder

F2B.currentWait: 6-7 years from petition to visa availability
F2B.pathToGreenCard: This leads directly to green card

pros.p1: Leads to permanent residence

cons.c1: 6-7 year wait
cons.c3: If petitioner naturalizes, moves to F-1 (may be longer wait)

F3.fullName: F-3: Married Children of U.S. Citizens
F3.purpose: For married children of any age of U.S. citizens

eligibility.e1: Married
eligibility.e2: Any age
eligibility.e3: Child of U.S. citizen

F3.currentWait: 12-13 years from petition to visa availability
F3.pathToGreenCard: This leads directly to green card for entire family

pros.p1: Includes spouse and children

cons.c1: 12-13 year wait for most countries

F4.fullName: F-4: Brothers and Sisters of U.S. Citizens
F4.purpose: For siblings of U.S. citizens (petitioner must be 21+)

eligibility.e1: Sibling of U.S. citizen
eligibility.e2: Petitioner must be 21+ years old
eligibility.e3: Includes half-siblings and step-siblings

F4.pathToGreenCard: This leads directly to green card for entire family

pros.p1: Includes spouse and all unmarried children under 21

cons.c1: Longest wait of all categories (13-24 years)
cons.c2: India: 15+ years, Mexico: 23+ years, Philippines: 24+ years
cons.c3: Many proposals to eliminate this category

STUDENT.purpose: For academic study at U.S. universities, colleges, high schools, or language programs

eligibility.e1: Accepted to SEVP-certified school
eligibility.e2: Demonstrate financial support
eligibility.e3: Maintain full course load

STUDENT.duration: Duration of study (D/S) plus 60-day grace period
STUDENT.pathToGreenCard: No direct path — but can change to H-1B through employer after graduation
STUDENT.spouseWork: F-2 spouse cannot work or study

pros.p1: Can work on campus
pros.p2: OPT allows 1-3 years work after graduation
pros.p4: Can apply for H-1B while on OPT

cons.c1: Must maintain full-time enrollment
cons.c3: Spouse cannot work

J1.purpose: For educational and cultural exchange programs

eligibility.e1: Accepted to designated exchange program
eligibility.e2: Categories: student, research, teacher, au pair, etc.
eligibility.e3: Must have program sponsor

J1.pathToGreenCard: Complicated — many subject to 2-year home residency requirement
J1.spouseWork: J-2 spouse can apply for work permit

pros.p1: Wide variety of programs

cons.c1: Many subject to 2-year home rule
cons.c2: Must return home for 2 years before H/L/green card
cons.c3: Waiver process complex and uncertain

M1.purpose: For full-time vocational or technical training programs (trade schools, flight schools, culinary arts, mechanics) — distinct from F-1 academic study

eligibility.e1: Accepted to a SEVP-certified vocational or non-academic institution
eligibility.e2: Enrolled full-time (at least 18 hours/week, or 22 hours for shop/lab programs)
eligibility.e3: Sufficient funds for entire program duration
eligibility.e4: Adequate English proficiency for the program
eligibility.e5: Intent to depart U.S. after program completion (non-immigrant intent)

M1.duration: 1 year initially, extendable in 1-year increments up to 3 years total, plus 30-day grace period
M1.currentWait: 2-3 months for visa interview at most consulates
M1.pathToGreenCard: No direct path — most M-1 students return home after training. Switch to other status (F-1, H-1B) is restricted.
M1.spouseWork: M-2 spouse cannot work or pursue full-time study

pros.p1: Allows specialized vocational training not available in home country
pros.p2: Lower SEVIS fee than F-1 ($220 vs $350)
pros.p3: Family can accompany on M-2 derivative visas

cons.c1: Cannot work on-campus or off-campus during studies
cons.c2: Practical training only allowed AFTER program completion (not during)
cons.c3: Cannot transfer to F-1 academic program easily — must apply for change of status
cons.c4: 3-year maximum total duration — cannot pursue multi-year programs
cons.c5: Spouse cannot work, limiting family financial flexibility

M1.note: M-1 is for vocational training only. If you want academic study (degree programs, language schools), F-1 is the right visa.

f1.name: Form I-20
f1.purpose: Certificate of Eligibility from SEVP-certified school

f2.name: SEVIS I-901
f2.who: Student pays online before visa interview

N400_5YR.purpose: Standard path to U.S. citizenship for green card holders after 5 years of continuous residence

eligibility.e1: Held green card for at least 5 years
eligibility.e2: Continuous residence in the U.S. for 5 years
eligibility.e3: Physical presence for at least 30 of the past 60 months
eligibility.e4: No single trip abroad longer than 6 months
eligibility.e6: Good moral character for the full 5-year period
eligibility.e7: Ability to read, write, and speak basic English

N400_5YR.duration: Permanent — you become a U.S. citizen for life
N400_5YR.currentWait: 8–14 months from filing to oath ceremony
N400_5YR.pathToGreenCard: N/A — this is the step after the green card
N400_5YR.spouseWork: As a U.S. citizen, your spouse becomes an Immediate Relative — fastest family sponsorship category

pros.p1: Vote in federal elections
pros.p2: U.S. passport — visa-free travel to 180+ countries
pros.p3: Sponsor immediate relatives with no quota wait
pros.p5: Eligible for federal jobs and security clearances
pros.p6: Children under 18 may automatically acquire citizenship

cons.c1: Must renounce prior citizenship (some countries allow dual — check yours)
cons.c3: Must pass English and civics tests
cons.c4: Prior violations or criminal history can disqualify

N400_5YR.note: You can file Form N-400 up to 90 days before you reach your 5-year eligibility date.

f2.name: Form I-90 (if needed)
f2.purpose: Renew green card if expired before naturalization
f2.who: Applicant files if GC is expired or expiring

N400_3YR.purpose: Expedited naturalization path for green card holders married to and living with a U.S. citizen

eligibility.e1: Held green card for at least 3 years
eligibility.e2: Currently married to the same U.S. citizen who petitioned for you
eligibility.e3: U.S. citizen spouse has been a citizen for all 3 years
eligibility.e4: Living together with citizen spouse at time of filing
eligibility.e5: Physical presence for at least 18 of the past 36 months
eligibility.e6: No single trip abroad longer than 6 months

N400_3YR.currentWait: 8–14 months from filing to oath ceremony
N400_3YR.pathToGreenCard: N/A — this is the step after the green card
N400_3YR.spouseWork: Once you naturalize, you can immediately sponsor other family members as a U.S. citizen

pros.p1: Eligible 2 years earlier than the standard 5-year route

cons.c1: Must still be married to and living with same citizen spouse
cons.c2: If marriage ends before filing, must wait for 5-year route
cons.c3: Divorce or separation can disqualify you from this route

N400_3YR.note: You must still be married to the same U.S. citizen at the time you file AND at the time of your interview.

f2.name: Marriage Certificate
f2.purpose: Proof of valid marriage to U.S. citizen

f3.name: Proof of Spouse's Citizenship
f3.purpose: U.S. passport or birth certificate of citizen spouse

N400_MIL.purpose: Expedited or immediate naturalization for active duty service members and honorably discharged veterans

eligibility.e1: Active duty or honorably discharged from U.S. armed forces
eligibility.e2: 1 year of honorable service during peacetime
eligibility.e3: Immediate eligibility during designated periods of hostility
eligibility.e5: Pass English and civics requirements (some exemptions apply)

N400_MIL.currentWait: Expedited — can be processed faster than civilian route
N400_MIL.pathToGreenCard: N/A — can apply even without a green card in some cases
N400_MIL.spouseWork: As a U.S. citizen, you can sponsor immediate family members with no wait

pros.p1: N-400 filing fee is completely waived ($0)
pros.p2: Can naturalize abroad through U.S. embassy
pros.p4: Some English/civics test exemptions for long-serving members
pros.p5: Posthumous citizenship available for those who die in service

cons.c1: Must have honorable discharge — dishonorable discharge disqualifies

N400_MIL.note: Filing fee is $0 for all active duty and honorably discharged veterans. Use Form N-426 if filing while serving abroad.

f1.name: Form N-400
f1.purpose: Application for Naturalization ($0 fee for military)

f2.name: Form N-426
f2.purpose: Request for Certification of Military or Naval Service

f3.name: DD-214
f3.purpose: Certificate of Release or Discharge from Active Duty

N600.purpose: Documents U.S. citizenship for children who acquired it automatically when a parent naturalized

eligibility.e1: Under 18 years old
eligibility.e2: Lawful permanent resident (green card holder)
eligibility.e3: At least one parent is a U.S. citizen by birth or naturalization
eligibility.e4: Residing in the U.S. in legal and physical custody of citizen parent

N600.currentWait: Varies — this documents citizenship that already occurred
N600.pathToGreenCard: N/A — child is already a citizen

pros.p1: May have already happened automatically — N-600 just proves it
pros.p2: Child gets U.S. passport and all citizen rights
pros.p3: Protects against deportation as an adult

cons.c1: $1,170 filing fee (highest citizenship form fee)
cons.c3: Does not apply if child was born after parent already naturalized

N600.note: If your parent naturalized while you were under 18 and you had a green card, you may ALREADY be a U.S. citizen — N-600 just documents it. Check before applying!

f1.name: Form N-600
f1.who: Parent files on behalf of child (or adult child files themselves)

f2.name: Parent's Naturalization Certificate
f2.purpose: Proof parent is a U.S. citizen

f3.name: Child's Green Card
f3.purpose: Proof of LPR status at time of parent's naturalization

ASYLUM.purpose: Protection for individuals already in the U.S. who fear persecution in their home country based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group

eligibility.e1: Physically present in the United States
eligibility.e2: Have suffered or fear future persecution on a protected ground
eligibility.e3: File Form I-589 within 1 year of arrival (limited exceptions)
eligibility.e4: Persecution must be by government, or groups government cannot/will not control
eligibility.e5: Not barred by criminal history, prior persecution of others, or firm resettlement

ASYLUM.duration: Asylum status is granted indefinitely — eligible to apply for green card after 1 year
ASYLUM.currentWait: USCIS affirmative cases: 6 months to 5+ years; immigration court (defensive): often 3-7 years
ASYLUM.pathToGreenCard: Apply for green card 1 year after asylum is granted (Form I-485)
ASYLUM.spouseWork: Spouse and children under 21 can be included as derivatives if in U.S. at filing

pros.p1: Path to permanent residency and eventually citizenship
pros.p2: Can apply for work authorization (EAD) after 150 days of pending application
pros.p3: Can include spouse and unmarried children under 21
pros.p4: Cannot be deported to country of feared persecution while case pending

cons.c1: $100 filing fee for Form I-589 (new as of 2025; was previously free)
cons.c2: Annual Asylum Fee (AAF) required for pending applications — failure to pay rejects application (effective May 29, 2026)
cons.c3: Strict 1-year filing deadline from arrival — exceptions are narrowly granted
cons.c4: EAD validity now 18 months max; auto-extensions eliminated as of Oct 2025
cons.c5: Policy environment is volatile — enhanced screening, country-specific pauses, increased detention
cons.c6: If denied affirmatively, case referred to immigration court for further proceedings
cons.c7: Frivolous applications can result in permanent bar from any U.S. immigration benefit

ASYLUM.note: ⚠️ Asylum policy is changing rapidly in 2026. Strongly recommended to consult an immigration attorney or accredited representative — many legal aid organizations offer free help.

f1.name: Form I-589
f1.purpose: Application for Asylum and for Withholding of Removal ($100 fee)
f1.who: Applicant files with USCIS or immigration court

f2.name: Form I-765 (c)(8)
f2.purpose: Employment Authorization based on pending asylum (after 150 days)

f3.name: Form I-485
f3.purpose: Adjustment to permanent resident (1 year after asylum granted)

f4.name: Form I-730
f4.purpose: Refugee/Asylee Relative Petition — bring spouse/children abroad
f4.who: Asylee files within 2 years of grant

REFUGEE.purpose: Protection for individuals OUTSIDE the U.S. who cannot return home due to persecution; processed through UNHCR and USRAP

eligibility.e1: Outside the United States
eligibility.e2: Of special humanitarian concern to the U.S.
eligibility.e3: Fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or social group
eligibility.e4: Generally referred by UNHCR, U.S. embassy, or designated NGO
eligibility.e5: Not firmly resettled in another country
eligibility.e6: Pass extensive security and medical screening

REFUGEE.duration: Refugee status leads to permanent residence — must apply for green card 1 year after arrival
REFUGEE.currentWait: 18-24 months from referral to U.S. arrival; processing remains slow
REFUGEE.pathToGreenCard: Required to apply for green card after 1 year in the U.S. (Form I-485)
REFUGEE.spouseWork: Authorized to work immediately upon arrival; spouse and children can come as derivatives

pros.p1: Travel and resettlement costs covered by U.S. government and resettlement agencies
pros.p2: Initial benefits including housing assistance, English classes, employment support
pros.p3: Work authorized immediately on arrival — no separate EAD needed
pros.p4: Path to permanent residency and citizenship
pros.p5: Family members can join through derivative status

cons.c1: Annual U.S. refugee admissions ceiling has been historically low and politically volatile
cons.c3: Limited control over which U.S. city you'll be resettled in
cons.c4: Cannot self-apply — must be referred through UNHCR or designated agency
cons.c5: Country-specific pauses and program restrictions are common in current policy environment

REFUGEE.note: ⚠️ The U.S. refugee program has been heavily restricted since 2025. Contact UNHCR (help.unhcr.org) for current intake status — most processing is currently paused or slowed for many countries.

f1.name: Form I-590
f1.who: Completed during overseas processing with USCIS officer

f2.name: Form I-485
f2.purpose: Adjustment to permanent resident (1 year after arrival)
f2.who: Refugee files after 1 year in U.S.

f3.name: Form I-730
f3.purpose: Refugee/Asylee Relative Petition — for family members abroad
f3.who: Refugee files within 2 years of arrival

content.c3: Certain student visa holders with job offers

dmv.title: DMV License Guide
dmv.description: Getting a driver's license as an immigrant

content.c1: Valid immigration status (requirements vary by state)
content.c5: Pass written test, vision test, and road test

content.c1: 19 states + DC allow licenses regardless of status
content.c2: California, New York, Illinois, New Jersey among them
content.c3: Some states issue 'standard' vs 'REAL ID' licenses
content.c4: REAL ID required for domestic flights starting May 2025
content.c5: Check your state's DMV website for specific requirements

content.c1: Employer-sponsored insurance (most common for work visa holders)
content.c2: ACA Marketplace plans (Healthcare.gov) — available to lawful residents
content.c3: Medicaid — varies by state and immigration status

content.c4: Emergency Medicaid available regardless of status
content.c5: Community health centers offer sliding-scale fees
content.c6: Healthcare.gov open enrollment: Nov 1 – Jan 15 annually

tax.title: Tax Filing for Immigrants
tax.description: Federal and state tax obligations for immigrants

content.c1: All U.S. residents (green card holders, substantial presence)
content.c2: H-1B, L-1, O-1 holders — taxed as residents
content.c3: F-1 students — generally nonresident for first 5 years

content.c5: Tax treaties may reduce obligations for some countries

travelProclamation.disclaimer: Entry restrictions change frequently and are subject to litigation. Verify current status at travel.state.gov before relying on this.

FULL_BAN.label: Entry currently suspended
FULL_BAN.description: Both immigrant and nonimmigrant visa issuance is suspended for nationals of this country, with limited exceptions (e.g. current green-card holders, existing valid visas).

PARTIAL_BAN.label: Entry partially restricted
PARTIAL_BAN.description: Immigrant visas and B/F/M/J visas are suspended for nationals of this country. Some other categories may still be possible — confirm your situation.

NONE.label: No current entry restriction
NONE.description: This country is not currently named in the entry proclamation. Status can change — verify before relying on it.

reasons.fullBan: This pathway requires a visa issued abroad, which is currently suspended for your country of citizenship. Exceptions are limited — speak with an attorney.
reasons.fullBanHumanitarian: This is a U.S.-based protection, so the entry suspension may not block it directly — but it does affect relatives abroad and re-entry. Confirm with an attorney.
reasons.partialBanImmigrant: Immigrant visas are currently suspended for your country of citizenship, which blocks this green-card pathway through a consulate. Limited exceptions apply.
reasons.partialBanHumanitarian: This is a U.S.-based protection. The partial entry restriction affects relatives abroad and re-entry rather than this filing directly.
reasons.partialBanCaution: Your country faces partial entry restrictions. This pathway is not categorically blocked, but processing may be affected — verify before proceeding.
reasons.immigrantVisaPause: Immigrant visa issuance for your country is currently paused. This green-card pathway may be delayed — verify current status.
reasons.adjudicationHold: Applications from your country may be subject to a processing hold. Expect possible delays and verify current status.

immediateRelativeAlert.dialogBody:
Immediate Relatives are the FASTEST family category because they have NO annual limits!

WHO QUALIFIES:
• Spouse of U.S. citizen
• Unmarried children under 21 of U.S. citizen
• Parents of U.S. citizen (if citizen is 21+)

WHY IT'S SPECIAL:
• No waiting for visa numbers
• No annual caps
• Process in 12-24 months vs 5-20 years

All other family categories must wait years for visa numbers to become available.
immediateRelativeAlert.bannerText: Spouse, minor children, and parents of U.S. citizens. Tap to learn why this matters.

citizenshipAlert.dialogBody:
Becoming a U.S. citizen gives you rights and protections beyond a green card:

🗳️ VOTE in federal, state, and local elections
🛂 U.S. PASSPORT — visa-free travel to 180+ countries
👨‍👩‍👧 SPONSOR FAMILY immediately as Immediate Relatives
🛡️ CANNOT BE DEPORTED — permanent security
💼 FEDERAL JOBS & security clearances
👶 CHILDREN under 18 may automatically become citizens

Most green card holders are eligible after 5 years. Married to a U.S. citizen? You may qualify after just 3 years.
citizenshipAlert.bannerText: Passport, voting rights, family sponsorship & more. Tap to learn.

work.title: Work-Based Immigration
work.overview: Employment-based immigration allows foreign nationals to work in the United States. Most categories require employer sponsorship.

h1b.name: H-1B Specialty Occupation
h1b.description: For positions requiring specialized knowledge and a bachelor's degree or higher.

l1.name: L-1 Intracompany Transfer
l1.description: For employees of multinational companies transferring to a U.S. office.

requirements.r1: Worked for company for 1 year in past 3 years

o1.name: O-1 Extraordinary Ability
o1.description: For individuals with extraordinary ability in sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics.

eb.name: Employment-Based Green Cards
eb.description: Permanent residence through employment-based categories (EB-1, EB-2, EB-3).

family.title: Family-Based Immigration
family.overview: U.S. citizens and permanent residents can sponsor eligible family members. Immediate relatives have no annual limits.

immediate.name: Immediate Relatives
immediate.description: Spouses, unmarried children under 21, and parents of U.S. citizens. No annual limits.

requirements.r2: Met in person within last 2 years

f1.name: F-1: Unmarried Sons/Daughters of U.S. Citizens
f1.description: For unmarried children 21+ of U.S. citizens. Current wait: 7-8 years.

f2a.name: F-2A: Spouses/Children of Green Card Holders
f2a.description: For spouses and unmarried children under 21 of permanent residents. Current wait: 2-3 years.

f2b.name: F-2B: Unmarried Adult Children of Green Card Holders
f2b.description: For unmarried children 21+ of permanent residents. Current wait: 6-7 years.

f3.name: F-3: Married Children of U.S. Citizens
f3.description: For married children of U.S. citizens. Current wait: 12-13 years.

f4.name: F-4: Siblings of U.S. Citizens
f4.description: For brothers/sisters of U.S. citizens (petitioner must be 21+). Current wait: 13-24 years.

student.title: Student Pathway
student.overview: International students may study full-time at SEVP-certified U.S. institutions. F-1 students can work through OPT (12 months) and STEM OPT (24 additional months) after graduation.

f1_student.name: F-1 Academic Student
f1_student.description: Full-time study at a U.S. university or college. OPT allows 1-3 years of work after graduation.

j1.name: J-1 Exchange Visitor
j1.description: For exchange programs (research, teaching, au pair). Many subject to 2-year home residency requirement.

m1.name: M-1 Vocational Student
m1.description: For vocational or non-academic programs. Limited work options after completion.

protection.title: Humanitarian Protection
protection.overview: Protection pathways for individuals unable to return home safely. Asylum must be filed within 1 year of arrival in the U.S. Policy environment is currently volatile.

asylum.name: Asylum
asylum.description: For those with a well-founded fear of persecution. Must file within 1 year of arrival.

refugee.name: Refugee Status
refugee.description: For individuals referred by UNHCR or a U.S. embassy while outside the United States.

citizenship.title: Path to Citizenship
citizenship.overview: U.S. naturalization allows lawful permanent residents (green card holders) to become U.S. citizens. Most applicants are eligible after 5 years of continuous residence. Spouses of U.S. citizens may qualify after 3 years.

standard.name: 5-Year Continuous Residence
standard.description: The standard naturalization path for most green card holders after 5 years of continuous residence.

requirements.r1: Green card held for at least 5 years
requirements.r2: Continuous residence in the U.S. for 5 years
requirements.r3: Physical presence for at least 30 months of the 5 years
requirements.r4: No single trip abroad longer than 6 months
requirements.r7: Ability to read, write, and speak English

marriage.name: 3-Year Rule (Married to U.S. Citizen)
marriage.description: Expedited path for green card holders married to and living with a U.S. citizen. Eligible after 3 years instead of 5.

requirements.r1: Green card held for at least 3 years
requirements.r2: Currently married to and living with a U.S. citizen
requirements.r3: Spouse has been a U.S. citizen for all 3 years
requirements.r4: Physical presence for at least 18 months of the 3 years
requirements.r5: No single trip abroad longer than 6 months

military.name: Military Service
military.description: Expedited or immediate naturalization for active duty service members and honorably discharged veterans.

requirements.r1: Active duty or honorably discharged from U.S. armed forces
requirements.r3: Active duty during designated hostility period = immediate eligibility

children.name: Citizenship for Children
children.description: Children under 18 with a green card may automatically acquire citizenship when a parent naturalizes. N-600 documents this citizenship.

requirements.r3: At least one parent is a U.S. citizen (by birth or naturalization)
requirements.r4: Residing in the U.S. in legal and physical custody of citizen parent

countryTips.disclaimer: General information, not legal advice. Rules change often — verify your situation with a licensed immigration attorney.
countryTips.proclamationGenericBody: Your country of citizenship is currently subject to a visa processing pause or hold. Verify current status at travel.state.gov before relying on a pathway.

general.title: Options are limited and changing
general.body: U.S. immigration pathways for Cuban nationals are heavily restricted and change frequently. Check the pathway section for how current restrictions affect each route, and consult a licensed immigration attorney before filing.

caa.title: Cuban Adjustment Act
caa.body: If you are Cuban and already in the U.S., after one year and one day of physical presence you may be able to apply for a green card under the Cuban Adjustment Act. This route is the subject of ongoing policy debate — confirm it still applies before relying on it.

general.title: Options are limited and changing
general.body: U.S. immigration pathways for Haitian nationals are heavily restricted and change frequently. Check the pathway section for how current restrictions affect each route, and consult a licensed immigration attorney before filing.

tps.title: Temporary Protected Status (TPS) — Ending
tps.body: On June 25, 2026, the Supreme Court ruled that courts cannot review the decision to end TPS. Haiti's designation is terminated and TPS-based work permits are ending. USCIS is extending the expiration date only in short increments, so it is changing week to week — do not rely on a date you saw before, or on the date printed on your card. Check the official USCIS TPS Haiti page for the current date, and speak with an immigration attorney or accredited representative now about other options. New initial TPS registration is not being accepted.

general.title: Common routes for Jamaican nationals
general.body: Jamaica has an E-2 investor treaty with the U.S., alongside the usual family- and employment-based routes. Which one fits depends on your situation — compare options in the pathway section.

e2.title: E-2 Treaty Investor option
e2.body: Jamaica holds an E-2 treaty with the U.S., so a substantial business investment can support an E-2 visa. There is no direct green-card path from E-2.

dv.title: Diversity Visa eligible
dv.body: Trinidad & Tobago is eligible for the DV Lottery for the current cycle. The official registration fee is $1 — anyone charging more is a scam.

e2.title: E-2 Treaty Investor option
e2.body: Trinidad & Tobago holds an E-2 treaty, so a substantial business investment can support an E-2 visa. There is no direct green-card path from E-2.

family.title: Family-based is the main route
family.body: For most people from the Dominican Republic, a petition from a U.S. citizen or permanent-resident relative is the primary path. The Dominican Republic is not eligible for the DV Lottery.

general.title: Common routes for Grenadian nationals
general.body: Grenada has an E-2 investor treaty with the U.S. (including for citizens by investment, with conditions), plus standard family- and employment-based routes. Compare options in the pathway section.

e2cbi.title: E-2 Treaty Investor option
e2cbi.body: Grenada holds an E-2 treaty. Citizenship obtained through Grenada's investment program requires three years of residence in Grenada before applying for E-2 (AMIGOS Act, 2022). Investment-based citizenship programs are under increased U.S. scrutiny, so treaty access through them carries policy risk.

territory.title: U.S. territory — you may already be a citizen
territory.body: People born in this U.S. territory are U.S. citizens at birth and generally do not need an immigration pathway. You can live and work anywhere in the U.S.
